title: Vanishing of the $\mu$-invariant of $p$-adic Hecke $L$ functions
speaker: Haruzo Hida

proof_stages.label: Katz multivariable CM p-adic L-function and mu target
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up an ordinary CM field and CM type, defines the diamond-bracket Hecke characters with weight variables, recalls Katz's multivariable p-adic Hecke L-function and its interpolation property, and formulates the goal that its p-adic mu-invariant is zero.

proof_stages.label: Imaginary-quadratic Eisenstein realization of CM L-values
proof_stages.summary: For exposition the argument is reduced to an imaginary quadratic field, where modified integral Eisenstein series are interpreted as geometric modular forms on elliptic curves and their CM evaluations at ideal lattices, after period normalization, give partial Hecke L-values.

proof_stages.label: Taylor/q-expansion criterion and initial obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The naive plan is to identify the Katz power series with a Serre-Tate or Taylor expansion at CM points and use a nonzero q-expansion mod p to force mu zero, but the speaker isolates two obstructions: an unwanted differential-operator factor and the need to evaluate at several ideal-class CM points.

proof_stages.label: Shimura action and Serre-Tate normalization
proof_stages.summary: Adelic automorphisms coming from the regular representation of the CM field move all relevant CM ideal points to a fixed base point, while the Serre-Tate coordinate at that point makes the torus action act by the anticyclotomic factor alpha^{1-c}, canceling the unwanted factor and producing the required local expansion of Katz's p-adic L-function.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic independence from Hecke-invariant subvarieties
proof_stages.summary: An independence theorem asserts that the transformed local expansions attached to distinct ring-class representatives are algebraically independent mod p; its proof turns any algebraic relation into a torus-stable closed subvariety of a product of Shimura-curve components and then uses a Chai-type Hecke-invariant subvariety result to obtain a class-group contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein q-expansion nonvanishing and mu equals zero
proof_stages.summary: Applying the independence theorem to the integral Eisenstein series reduces the vanishing of the mu-invariant to q-expansion nonvanishing, which follows from elementary Eisenstein coefficients such as 1+ell^{k-1} not being divisible by p.

proof_stages.label: General CM/Hilbert refinements and specialization phenomena
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that in non-imaginary-quadratic Hilbert modular cases some one-variable or anticyclotomic specializations can have positive mu because of support issues, but varying in the full cyclotomic and anticyclotomic variables still gives the desired multivariable vanishing.

examples.name: Hypersymmetric point on a unitary Shimura variety
examples.note: The speaker mentions a hypersymmetric point, such as a product of copies of one elliptic curve in a unitary Shimura variety, only to illustrate an easier case of the Hecke-invariant subvariety principle.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites hypersymmetric points (e.g. for U(n,n) as a product of copies of one elliptic curve with large endomorphism ring) as an easier case, without carrying out an argument on that example.

passes.reason: A hypersymmetric Shimura point has unusually large endomorphism symmetry, but is used as an easy illustrative case rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point has unusually large endomorphism symmetry, here used as an easier illustrative case rather than to refute a claim.
a hypersymmetric Shimura point has maximal endomorphism symmetry and is cited as an easier case, not as a failure case
A hypersymmetric Shimura point has unusually large/maximal endomorphisms, and here it is cited as an easy symmetric case rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point has unusually large endomorphism symmetry, and it is cited as an easy illustrative case rather than to refute a claim.
Hypersymmetric Shimura points have unusually large endomorphism symmetry and are cited as an easier case rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point has maximal endomorphism/symmetry, and is cited as an easy case rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point is invoked as an easy highly endomorphic case, not to refute a claim.
It is cited as an easier highly endomorphic Shimura point, not as a refutation or obstruction.
hypersymmetric Shimura points have maximal endomorphism symmetry and are cited only as an easy special case.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point such as a power of one elliptic curve has unusually large endomorphisms and hence maximal symmetry, and is used as an easy proving case rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersymmetric Shimura point such as a power of one elliptic curve has unusually large endomorphism/symmetry and is used as an easier illustrative case, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Everywhere-ramified CM extension causing positive specialized mu
examples.note: In the Hilbert modular generalization, the example of a CM extension ramified at every finite place exhibits a support obstruction that explains why line or anticyclotomic specializations may have positive mu and motivates using the full multivariable family.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the everywhere-ramified CM case by explaining how ramification makes the automorphic induction lack full support, how this can give positive specialized mu, and how varying by p-power roots of unity avoids divisibility in the full multivariable function.

passes.reason: An everywhere-ramified CM extension is a maximally ramified special case used to show that a specialized mu-invariant can be positive, contrary to naive specialized vanishing.
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is an extreme ramification case used to show specialized mu-invariants can be positive, obstructing vanishing after specialization.
the everywhere-ramified CM extension is an extreme ramification case used to show specialized mu-invariants can be positive despite the full multivariable vanishing result
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is a maximal-ramification case used to show specialized mu-invariants can be positive, so it obstructs naive specialized vanishing.
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is a maximal-ramification edge case used to show that specialized anticyclotomic mu-invariants can be positive.
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is a maximal ramification case used to show that specialized mu-invariants can be positive.
The everywhere-ramified CM extension is used to show that a specialized μ-invariant can be positive, with ramification at the maximal boundary of the relevant class.
The everywhere-ramified CM extension is used to show that specialized one-variable mu-invariants can be positive, so specialized vanishing fails.
It is used to show that a specialized mu-invariant can be positive despite the full multivariable mu vanishing, and it is maximally ramified in the stated setting.
the maximally ramified CM extension makes specialized μ positive, refuting vanishing after specialization.
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is an extreme ramification case invoked to show that a specialized mu-invariant can be positive, so it obstructs naive vanishing for specializations.
An everywhere-ramified CM extension is a maximally ramified case used to show that a specialized one-variable mu-invariant can be positive despite the intended multivariable vanishing.
